channel.title: void --onair: US Brief
channel.description: Twice-daily US news briefing. Two journalists, 150 curated US sources, every story scored for bias on six axes. No ads. No paywall.
channel.itunes:author: void --news

item.title: Gas Prices Hit $4 Amid Iran War — US Evening Brief
item.description:
US average gas prices reached $4 per gallon Tuesday. This marks the first time national prices have hit this level since August 2022. The surge in fuel costs follows the ongoing conflict with Iran, now in its second month. American consumers face immediate economic pressure from the escalating war. President Trump issued an executive order to pay Transportation Security Administration officers from the Immigration and Customs Enforcement budget. TSA agents now receive back pay, shortening airport security lines while the Department of Homeland Security remains unfunded by Congress. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth stated a regime change has occurred in Iran, urging Tehran to cut a deal with the United States. Hegseth's declaration comes as the Pentagon describes the "upcoming days" as decisive in the conflict. The Supreme Court prepares to hear arguments this week on President Trump's executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship, challenging automatic citizenship for children born in the U.S. to undocumented parents. Japan explores rerouting oil from Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan, a direct response to growing concerns about supply stability due to a de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. These domestic and international developments underscore a period of heightened executive action and constitutional challenge. Global energy markets react to geopolitical shifts, testing both American household budgets and the separation of powers.
Full bias analysis and source spectrum: https://aacrit.github.io/void--news/us/
item.itunes:summary: US average gas prices reached $4 per gallon Tuesday. This marks the first time national prices have hit this level since August 2022. The surge in fuel costs follows the ongoing conflict with Iran, now in its second month. American consumers face immediate economic pressure from the escalating war. President Trump issued an executive order to pay Transportation Security Administration officers from the Immigration and Customs Enforcement budget. TSA agents now receive back pay, shortening airport security lines while the Department of Homeland Security remains unfunded by Congress. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth stated a regime change has occurred in Iran, urging Tehran to cut a deal with the United States. Hegseth's declaration comes as the Pentagon describes the "upcoming days" as decisive in the conflict. The Supreme Court prepares to hear arguments this week on President Trump's executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship, challenging automatic citizenship for children born in the U.S. to undocumented parents. Japan explores rerouting oil from Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan, a direct response to growing concerns about supply stability due to a de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. These domestic and international developments underscore a period of heightened executive action and constitutional challenge. Global energy markets react to geopolitical shifts, testing both American household budgets and the separation of powers.

item.title: Defense Secretary Faces Investment Allegations — US Morning Brief
item.description:
A Financial Times report alleges Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's broker sought defense fund investment in February, prior to US-Israel strikes on Iran. The Pentagon calls the story "entirely false and fabricated," demanding a retraction. This allegation surfaces as six people were wounded in central Israel by Iranian missile strikes. Four Israeli soldiers died in fighting in southern Lebanon. Florida Governor Ron DeSantis signed a bill renaming Palm Beach International Airport after former President Donald J. Trump. The change takes effect July 1st, a symbolic gesture in a key political state. The White House urges Congress to suspend recess and fund the Department of Homeland Security. This signals escalating concern over border operations and federal funding. The US conveyed a message to Kyiv, urging Ukraine to scale back attacks on Russian oil facilities. This shift follows Moscow signaling distress over the strikes. Gulf allies, led by Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, are pressing President Trump to continue the war against Iran. They believe Tehran remains insufficiently weakened after a month-long bombing campaign. The FBI labeled the March 12 attack on Michigan's Temple Israel an act of terrorism, inspired by Hezbollah. Domestic political actions, allegations of financial impropriety, and escalating international conflicts now converge. Policy decisions carry immediate, visible consequences, both at home and abroad.
Full bias analysis and source spectrum: https://aacrit.github.io/void--news/us/
item.itunes:summary: A Financial Times report alleges Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's broker sought defense fund investment in February, prior to US-Israel strikes on Iran. The Pentagon calls the story "entirely false and fabricated," demanding a retraction. This allegation surfaces as six people were wounded in central Israel by Iranian missile strikes. Four Israeli soldiers died in fighting in southern Lebanon. Florida Governor Ron DeSantis signed a bill renaming Palm Beach International Airport after former President Donald J. Trump. The change takes effect July 1st, a symbolic gesture in a key political state. The White House urges Congress to suspend recess and fund the Department of Homeland Security. This signals escalating concern over border operations and federal funding. The US conveyed a message to Kyiv, urging Ukraine to scale back attacks on Russian oil facilities. This shift follows Moscow signaling distress over the strikes. Gulf allies, led by Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, are pressing President Trump to continue the war against Iran. They believe Tehran remains insufficiently weakened after a month-long bombing campaign. The FBI labeled the March 12 attack on Michigan's Temple Israel an act of terrorism, inspired by Hezbollah. Domestic political actions, allegations of financial impropriety, and escalating international conflicts now converge. Policy decisions carry immediate, visible consequences, both at home and abroad.

item.title: US Brief — March 31, 2026 (Morning)
item.description:
Spain denied US military aircraft access to its airspace and bases Tuesday, citing the "illegal" nature of the Iran conflict. This decision directly impacts US logistical operations in the region, forcing immediate rerouting and potentially complicating supply lines for ongoing military actions. Spanish Defense Minister Margarita Robles publicly confirmed the move, marking Spain as Europe's most critical voice regarding US and Israeli military actions. Secretary of State Marco Rubio stated US war objectives in Iran would be achieved "within weeks," an assessment offered as Iran launched long-range ballistic missiles. Two missiles targeted the joint UK-US army base in Diego Garcia, 4000 km away, demonstrating an extended strike radius. Turkey reported NATO defenses shot down another Iranian missile after it entered Turkish airspace, the fourth such incident. President Trump threatened to destroy Iran's oil export hub and power plants if a peace deal is not reached soon. The White House would not rule out threats to Iran's water supply, a potential violation of the Geneva Convention. Domestically, the FBI identified the Michigan synagogue attack as a "Hezbollah-inspired act of terrorism," confirming the attacker purposely targeted the Jewish community and planned the attack for days. These developments underscore the expanding reach of the conflict, from European airspace to American communities, raising questions about the broader strategic costs.
Full bias analysis and source spectrum: https://aacrit.github.io/void--news/us/
item.itunes:summary: Spain denied US military aircraft access to its airspace and bases Tuesday, citing the "illegal" nature of the Iran conflict. This decision directly impacts US logistical operations in the region, forcing immediate rerouting and potentially complicating supply lines for ongoing military actions. Spanish Defense Minister Margarita Robles publicly confirmed the move, marking Spain as Europe's most critical voice regarding US and Israeli military actions. Secretary of State Marco Rubio stated US war objectives in Iran would be achieved "within weeks," an assessment offered as Iran launched long-range ballistic missiles. Two missiles targeted the joint UK-US army base in Diego Garcia, 4000 km away, demonstrating an extended strike radius. Turkey reported NATO defenses shot down another Iranian missile after it entered Turkish airspace, the fourth such incident. President Trump threatened to destroy Iran's oil export hub and power plants if a peace deal is not reached soon. The White House would not rule out threats to Iran's water supply, a potential violation of the Geneva Convention. Domestically, the FBI identified the Michigan synagogue attack as a "Hezbollah-inspired act of terrorism," confirming the attacker purposely targeted the Jewish community and planned the attack for days. These developments underscore the expanding reach of the conflict, from European airspace to American communities, raising questions about the broader strategic costs.
